title: ‘Monkey cap burglar’ linked to 150 thefts and accomplice nabbed by Wayanad police after long hunt
content: Kalpetta: A burglar accused in over 150 theft cases across Kerala and Tamil Nadu has been arrested along with his accomplice after a months-long manhunt by the police. The accused has been identified as Kollamparambil K U Muhammed, 46, a native of Alakkode in Kannur district, and his accomplice K T Jose alias Kambalakkad Jose, 72, from Padichira near Pulppally. The duo is suspected to be behind a series of thefts across several districts in the Malabar region and parts of Tamil Nadu's Nilgiris. Police said Muhammed used to mask his face to evade CCTV surveillance and primarily targeted houses left unattended. The Special Investigation Team (SIT), led by District Superintendent of Police Arun K Pavithran, was constituted after investigators failed to crack a spate of housebreaking cases in the district. Muhammed was arrested from Poovanchal near Alakkode in Kannur, while Jose was nabbed from Boys' Town near Mananthavady. During interrogation, the accused confessed to multiple thefts in areas including Madakkimala, Muttil, Meppadi, Meenangadi, Padanthara and Cherambadi in the Nilgiris. Police first arrested the duo in connection with a house theft at Gramathuvayal near Kalpetta, where around nine sovereigns of gold were stolen. The accused had broken open the lock and forced open a locker shelf. Following the incident, police registered a case and deployed dog squads, fingerprint experts and forensic teams. CCTV footage from nearby areas was examined, and intelligence was gathered on suspicious movements in jewellery outlets. The sustained investigation and surveillance eventually led to the arrests. Police said Muhammed had carried out two more thefts after the Gramathuvayal incident, while Jose allegedly helped by identifying houses left unattended. Muhammed was apprehended while travelling in an Innova car (KL 11 BB 8709). Police seized gold ornaments, tools used for theft and cash from the vehicle. Despite intensive efforts, it took months for the police to track down the accused. The investigation team was divided into multiple groups, and surveillance was strengthened at public functions, large weddings and jewellery shops. Officers also went undercover to gather intelligence on the movement of habitual offenders. Modus operandi Six feet tall, dressed in a full-sleeve T-shirt, tracksuit and a monkey cap that concealed most of his face, Muhammed rarely revealed his identity. To evade CCTV cameras, he would park his vehicle in nearby towns and walk to his targets through the rear of houses and across farmlands. According to police, the total quantity of gold stolen by Muhammed from areas under Sulthan Bathery, Meenangadi and Kalpetta police station limits alone exceeded 200 sovereigns. He is also said to own a large house in his native place, Alakkode. Muhammed would identify target houses in advance, often striking during local temple festivals and church feasts when families were away. At Muttil near Kalpetta, he carried out one of his biggest thefts, stealing around 80 sovereigns of gold. Police said Muhammed began operating extensively in Wayanad from 2023. He had been arrested earlier, but after being released on parole, he expanded his operations to Nilgiris district in Tamil Nadu. Investigators believe he would attempt up to a dozen break-ins in a single night, succeeding in three or four and escaping with large quantities of gold and cash. His presence in an area would leave residents anxious, with many spending sleepless nights fearing another strike. The accused were produced before the Judicial First Class Magistrate Court in Mananthavady and remanded to judicial custody. Kalpetta Station House Officer T P Dinesh said more cases linked to the duo are likely to emerge during further interrogation. Police will seek their custody for detailed questioning...
